stem: A 6-year-old boy (weight 22 kg) with known peanut allergy is brought to a tertiary ED by ambulance 25 minutes after biting a satay skewer at a family function. Within minutes he developed urticaria, lip swelling, vomiting, and audible stridor. He received IM adrenaline 300 microg into the lateral thigh by his parents at the scene and a second IM adrenaline 300 microg by paramedics 6 minutes later, with two boluses of 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg en route. On arrival he is given high-flow oxygen 15 L/min via non-rebreather and a nebulised adrenaline 5 mg has just been started for persistent stridor. He has had no previous anaphylaxis admission and is not on beta-blockers. Despite these measures he remains hypotensive with ongoing wheeze, persistent stridor, and a flushed mottled appearance ten minutes after the second IM dose. The PICU registrar is en route.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158 bpm, RR 38 /min, SpO2 91% on 15 L/min non-rebreather, BP 72/40 mmHg, T 36.8 deg C
data_table.Examination: Diffuse urticaria, marked lip and periorbital oedema, biphasic stridor, widespread expiratory wheeze, capillary refill 4 seconds, GCS 14 (drowsy but rousable)
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.29, lactate 4.4 mmol/L, K 3.6 mmol/L, glucose 6.1 mmol/L; tryptase taken; ECG sinus tachycardia
data_table.Medications received: IM adrenaline 300 microg x2, 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg x2, nebulised adrenaline 5 mg in progress, high-flow oxygen
data_table.History: Peanut allergy with prior urticaria only, no asthma, no beta-blocker use, fully immunised

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and IV chlorphenamine 2.5 mg, then reassess at 15 minutes
options.rationale: Steroids and antihistamines do not treat the airway or circulatory collapse of anaphylaxis and have no role as the next escalation when adrenaline has failed, as per ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines.
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Give a third IM adrenaline 300 microg into the lateral thigh and repeat every 5 minutes indefinitely
options.rationale: After two or more IM doses without adequate response, refractory anaphylaxis is declared and an IV adrenaline infusion is preferred over ongoing IM dosing for sustained titratable effect, as per ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines and ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.7.
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Commence an IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min titrated to BP, with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis (persistent hypotension or airway compromise after at least two IM doses) is treated with a titrated IV adrenaline infusion starting at 0.1 microg/kg/min in a monitored area, with PICU involvement, as per ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines and RCH CPG: Anaphylaxis.
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Give IV glucagon 30 microg/kg as a bolus to overcome adrenaline-resistant shock from beta-blockade
options.rationale: Glucagon is used when anaphylaxis is refractory in patients taking beta-blockers; this child is not on beta-blockers and should receive an adrenaline infusion first, as per ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines.
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Give IV hydroxocobalamin 70 mg/kg for suspected concurrent cyanide-mediated vasodilation
options.rationale: Hydroxocobalamin is an antidote for cyanide toxicity (smoke inhalation) and has no role in anaphylaxis, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

explanation.summary: Refractory anaphylaxis is failure to respond to two or more IM adrenaline doses. Management is an IV adrenaline infusion in a monitored area (PICU/ED resus) alongside ongoing fluid resuscitation, oxygen, and airway preparation. Steroids and antihistamines are adjuncts, not next steps. Glucagon is reserved for beta-blocked patients.
key_points: Refractory anaphylaxis = persistent hypotension or airway compromise after >=2 IM adrenaline doses
Start IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min and titrate
Continuous cardiac monitoring is mandatory
Fluid resuscitation 20 mL/kg crystalloid boluses repeated as needed
Tryptase taken acutely and at 1-2 hours and at 24 hours
explanation.pearls: Stridor in anaphylaxis is laryngeal oedema, not bronchospasm; nebulised adrenaline buys time but does not replace IV adrenaline.

sources.label: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

stem: An 18-month-old girl (weight 11 kg) with no prior seizure history is brought to a regional ED in active generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began 28 minutes ago at home. Paramedics gave buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg at 5 minutes and a second buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg at 15 minutes without termination. On arrival at 25 minutes she remains in generalised convulsion with eye deviation, frothing, and incontinence; IV access has just been secured on the second attempt and a bedside glucose is 4.8 mmol/L. She had a brief febrile illness with rhinorrhoea over the preceding 48 hours and a temperature of 38.6 deg C recorded today, but there is no rash, no vomiting, and no known toxic ingestion accessible in the home. She has met all developmental milestones to date, is fully immunised to the current Australian schedule, and has no family history of epilepsy or sudden infant death. The mother denies head trauma and no household members are on antiepileptic or antidepressant medications. There has been no prior antiepileptic exposure. The team has applied high-flow oxygen at 8 L/min via Hudson mask, has charcoal-impregnated suction available at the bedside, has prepared a rapid-sequence induction trolley, and the anaesthetic registrar and PICU outreach team have been paged.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 28 /min (intermittent), SpO2 94% on 8 L/min Hudson mask, BP 96/58 mmHg, T 38.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic activity, GCS 3 during seizure, pupils 4 mm equal reactive, no neck stiffness assessable, no rash, anterior fontanelle closed
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.8 mmol/L, VBG pH 7.20 pCO2 56 mmHg lactate 5.8 mmol/L, Na 137 mmol/L, Ca 2.30 mmol/L, FBC and CRP pending
data_table.Medications: Buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg at 5 min and 15 min, no other antiepileptics ever
data_table.History: First seizure, febrile URTI 48 h, fully immunised, no trauma, no toxin

lead_in: Following the APLS status epilepticus algorithm, what is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes with continuous ECG monitoring
options.rationale: Phenytoin remains an acceptable second-line agent in older children, but the EcLiPSE and ConSEPT trials and the updated APLS Australia algorithm prefer levetiracetam, particularly in children under 2 years where phenytoin carries higher arrhythmia and infusion-site risk, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

options.text: IV phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes with airway support ready
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is the preferred second-line agent only in neonates; in infants and children outside the neonatal period it is third-line after levetiracetam (and after phenytoin if used), as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

options.text: A third dose of buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg and continue to observe for spontaneous resolution
options.rationale: More than two doses of benzodiazepine increases respiratory depression without improving termination rates; the algorithm advances to a second-line antiepileptic at this point, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg (max 3 g) over 5 minutes with continuous monitoring
options.rationale: After two benzodiazepine doses, the current APLS Australia and RCH algorithm recommends IV levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg as the preferred second-line agent in infants and children, supported by EcLiPSE/ConSEPT/ESETT data, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: Rapid sequence induction with thiopentone 4 mg/kg and intubation as primary seizure control
options.rationale: Thiopentone induction is reserved for refractory status epilepticus after second-line agents have failed; jumping to RSI before trialling a second-line antiepileptic skips the algorithm, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

explanation.summary: Convulsive status epilepticus algorithm: oxygen, glucose check, two doses of benzodiazepine 5 minutes apart, then second-line antiepileptic at ~25 minutes. Following EcLiPSE/ConSEPT/ESETT, levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg IV is the preferred second-line agent in children outside the neonatal period. If still seizing, proceed to RSI with thiopentone or midazolam infusion.
key_points: Two doses of benzodiazepine max before moving on
Levetiracetam preferred over phenytoin in young children
Phenobarbitone is for neonates as second-line
Refractory status -> thiopentone RSI and PICU
Always check glucose, sodium, and consider non-convulsive causes
explanation.pearls: Pyridoxine 100 mg IV should be considered in infants under 18 months with refractory seizures of unclear cause.

stem: A 9-year-old boy with severe persistent asthma (one PICU admission 18 months ago for life-threatening wheeze, baseline fluticasone-salmeterol BD via spacer, salbutamol PRN) is brought to a tertiary ED after 12 hours of progressive wheeze triggered by a viral URTI in the family. In ED over the past 60 minutes he has received continuous back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg every 20 minutes for three doses, oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg with antiemetic cover, and high-flow oxygen at 10 L/min via nebuliser mask. There has been no clinical improvement: he remains exhausted, leaning forward, speaking only in single words, and looks visibly tired between breaths. He has not yet received any IV bronchodilators, IV magnesium, or aminophylline. He is normotensive but markedly tachycardic with a fine tremor and palpitations from the salbutamol. There has been no fever, no foreign body history, no chest pain, and no preceding anaphylactic features. The PICU outreach team has reviewed at the bedside and recommends escalation but has not yet started any infusions while awaiting the ED decision. He has a 22-gauge cannula in the right antecubital fossa and a second cannula being placed; his last meal was 4 hours ago. There are no known drug allergies and parents have given verbal consent for further therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158 bpm, RR 42 /min, SpO2 88% on 10 L/min via nebuliser mask, BP 116/72 mmHg, T 37.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Tripoding, single-word speech, marked accessory muscle use, silent chest in upper zones, faint expiratory wheeze in lower zones, GCS 15 but visibly exhausted, fine tremor
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.30 pCO2 50 mmHg HCO3 22 mmol/L lactate 3.8 mmol/L, K 3.0 mmol/L, glucose 9.4 mmol/L; CXR hyperinflation, no pneumothorax
data_table.Medications: Continuous nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium x3 doses, oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg given 35 min ago, no IV bronchodilator yet
data_table.History: Previous PICU admission age 7.5, fluticasone-salmeterol BD baseline, no LABA-only, no NSAID use

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg every 20 minutes and reassess in 1 hour
options.rationale: He has already failed three back-to-back nebulised doses plus systemic steroid; persisting without escalation risks decompensation and is not the next step, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with continuous cardiac and BP monitoring
options.rationale: Severe acute asthma failing oxygen, back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium and systemic steroid escalates to IV magnesium sulfate before IV salbutamol or aminophylline, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute and Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: IV aminophylline 10 mg/kg loading over 60 minutes followed by 1 mg/kg/h infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is an option but is reserved for cases failing IV magnesium and IV salbutamol because of its narrow therapeutic index and arrhythmia risk, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: IV salbutamol 15 microg/kg over 10 minutes followed by 1 microg/kg/min infusion
options.rationale: IV salbutamol is reasonable but typically follows IV magnesium in the RCH escalation ladder; potassium is already 3.0 mmol/L and lactate 3.8 mmol/L which IV salbutamol will worsen, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Immediate rapid sequence intubation with ketamine 2 mg/kg and rocuronium 1 mg/kg
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma carries high risk of bronchospasm, hypotension, and barotrauma and is reserved for impending arrest after maximal medical therapy, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

explanation.summary: Severe paediatric asthma escalates: oxygen, back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium, systemic corticosteroid, then IV magnesium sulfate, then IV salbutamol, then IV aminophylline, with intubation as last resort.
key_points: IV magnesium dose: 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 min
Silent chest indicates life-threatening asthma
Watch for hypokalaemia and lactic acidosis from salbutamol
Aminophylline is last-line because of arrhythmia risk
Involve PICU early when failing nebs + steroid
explanation.pearls: Ketamine is the induction agent of choice if RSI cannot be avoided because of its bronchodilator effect.

stem: A 4-year-old boy (weight 16 kg) presents to a regional ED with 36 hours of fever and 6 hours of progressive lethargy following a varicella rash that began crusting 4 days ago. He has now had three 20 mL/kg boluses of 0.9% sodium chloride for hypotension and poor perfusion, with empirical IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and IV clindamycin given for suspected invasive group A streptococcal septic shock. Peripheral IV access only is available; the central line trolley has been requested but the local team is not credentialled to insert a femoral line, and the retrieval team is 45 minutes away. He remains shut down with cool mottled extremities to mid-thigh, prolonged capillary refill, narrow pulse pressure, and weak peripheral pulses. He has not yet received any inotrope or vasoactive infusion. Bedside echo by the retrieval consultant on tele-link shows a hyperdynamic underfilled left ventricle and no pericardial effusion. Lactate is rising despite ongoing resuscitation.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178 bpm, RR 36 /min, SpO2 96% on 6 L/min Hudson mask, BP 74/40 mmHg, T 39.1 deg C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 13, cool mottled peripheries to mid-thigh, capillary refill 5 seconds, weak radial pulses, no rash beyond crusted varicella
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.21 lactate 5.6 mmol/L base excess -10, Na 132 mmol/L, K 4.4 mmol/L, glucose 4.2 mmol/L, WCC 22 x10^9/L, CRP 248 mg/L, coagulation deranged
data_table.Fluid given: 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg x3 over 45 minutes; no albumin, no inotrope yet
data_table.Access: Two peripheral IV cannulae, no central line, intraosseous available

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in haemodynamic management?

options.text: Commence peripheral IV adrenaline infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min and titrate to perfusion
options.rationale: Cold septic shock in children (low cardiac output, high systemic vascular resistance) is treated with adrenaline as the first-line inotrope; when only peripheral access is available, dilute peripheral adrenaline is recommended over delay for central access, as per ANZICS Paediatric Septic Shock Guideline and Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines.
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric Advanced Life Support

options.text: Commence peripheral IV noradrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min as first-line vasopressor
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is first-line for warm shock (vasodilated, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure); this child has cold shock with vasoconstriction and low cardiac output, where adrenaline is preferred, as per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines.

options.text: Give a fourth 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride before any inotrope
options.rationale: After 40-60 mL/kg of crystalloid in septic shock without response, further boluses risk pulmonary oedema; inotropes should be started, as per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines and FEAST trial considerations.

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg as the sole next haemodynamic intervention
options.rationale: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is considered for catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency, but it is not the next step before any inotrope, as per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines.

options.text: Defer all inotrope infusion until a central venous line is inserted to avoid extravasation injury
options.rationale: Delaying inotrope for central access in fluid-refractory shock increases mortality; dilute peripheral or intraosseous adrenaline/noradrenaline is recommended until central access is achieved, as per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines.

explanation.summary: Paediatric septic shock that is fluid-refractory after 40-60 mL/kg of crystalloid requires early inotrope. Cold shock (low cardiac output, narrow pulse pressure, prolonged capillary refill) is treated with adrenaline; warm shock (vasodilated, wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses) is treated with noradrenaline. Peripheral or intraosseous infusion is appropriate while central access is being arranged.
key_points: Cold shock -> adrenaline; warm shock -> noradrenaline
Do not delay inotrope for central line if peripherally feasible
Stop fluid boluses once signs of fluid overload appear
Stress-dose hydrocortisone for catecholamine-resistant shock
Source control and broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour
explanation.pearls: Varicella plus septic shock equals invasive group A strep until proven otherwise; clindamycin is added to suppress toxin production.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric Advanced Life Support

stem: An 11-year-old girl (weight 38 kg) is admitted to PICU with new-onset diabetic ketoacidosis (initial venous pH 7.05, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, corrected sodium 141 mmol/L at admission). She received an initial 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride for shock and was then commenced on a deficit-plus-maintenance regimen calculated for 7% dehydration over 48 hours, with an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/h started 1 hour after the fluids and added potassium 40 mmol/L once she had urinated. She had no preceding head injury, no toxic ingestion, and no prior diabetes diagnosis; her family history is notable only for type 2 diabetes in a paternal uncle. Six hours into therapy the bedside nurse notes a 15-minute history of increasing headache, restlessness, and one episode of vomiting; over the past 20 minutes she has become more drowsy and confused, no longer engaging with her parents. Pre-decline GCS was 15; current GCS is 12 (E3 V3 M6). The most recent blood glucose is 14.2 mmol/L (was 32, falling at 3 mmol/L/h), sodium has fallen from 138 to 132 mmol/L despite the glucose correction (corrected sodium therefore falling), and she now has bradycardia and a rising blood pressure on the trend chart. No focal neurology has yet been demonstrated; pupils are equal at 3 mm and reactive. The on-call PICU consultant has been called and the bed is being prepared for hyperosmolar therapy and possible intubation.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 62 bpm (was 110), RR 18 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 138/82 mmHg (was 102/64), T 36.8 deg C
data_table.Examination: GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6), drowsy and irritable, no focal weakness, pupils 3 mm equal reactive, no papilloedema, Kussmaul breathing resolving
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 14.2 mmol/L, ketones 3.8 mmol/L, Na 132 mmol/L (corrected was 141 at admission, now ~134), K 3.6 mmol/L, pH 7.22, bicarbonate 14 mmol/L; head CT not yet done
data_table.Fluids/insulin: 0.9% saline + KCl 40 mmol/L at deficit + maintenance for 7% over 48 h, insulin 0.05 units/kg/h, no dextrose yet added
data_table.Trajectory: Glucose falling 3 mmol/L/h, corrected Na falling, GCS dropping over 20 minutes

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reduce insulin infusion to 0.025 units/kg/h and add 5% dextrose to current fluid bag, continue current rate
options.rationale: Adding dextrose and reducing insulin is appropriate when glucose falls below ~14-17 mmol/L but does not address suspected cerebral oedema with bradycardia, hypertension and falling GCS, which requires immediate hyperosmolar therapy, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines and RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Increase IV fluid rate to replace deficit over 24 hours instead of 48 hours to improve cerebral perfusion
options.rationale: Increasing fluid rate worsens cerebral oedema; current evidence (PECARN FLUID trial) supports cautious rehydration and immediate hyperosmolar therapy when oedema is suspected, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

options.text: Give IV hypertonic 3% sodium chloride 5 mL/kg over 10-15 minutes, reduce IV fluid rate by one-third, and arrange urgent head CT
options.rationale: Bradycardia, hypertension, falling GCS, headache and vomiting are the Cushing-like triad of DKA-related cerebral oedema; immediate hyperosmolar therapy with 3% saline 2.5-5 mL/kg (or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg) plus fluid rate reduction and head CT once stable are first-line, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines and RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Give IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for cerebral oedema and continue current fluid and insulin regimen
options.rationale: Dexamethasone reduces vasogenic oedema (e.g. tumour, meningitis) but has no role in DKA-related cytotoxic/osmotic cerebral oedema, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

options.text: Immediately intubate and hyperventilate to a target pCO2 of 25-28 mmHg to lower intracranial pressure
options.rationale: Aggressive prophylactic hyperventilation below pCO2 30 mmHg in DKA cerebral oedema is associated with worse outcomes; intubation may be needed but is not the first step before hyperosmolar therapy, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

explanation.summary: DKA-related cerebral oedema presents 4-12 hours into treatment with headache, vomiting, falling GCS, bradycardia and hypertension. Immediate management is hyperosmolar therapy (3% saline 2.5-5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg), reduction of fluid rate by one-third, head-up positioning, and urgent imaging only once stabilised. Avoid aggressive hyperventilation.
key_points: Risk factors: new diagnosis, age <5, severe acidosis, high urea, low pCO2, bicarbonate use, rapid fluid
Treat clinically; do not wait for imaging
3% saline 2.5-5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg
Reduce IV fluid rate; keep glucose falling no faster than ~5 mmol/L/h
Avoid pCO2 below 30 mmHg
explanation.pearls: Corrected sodium should rise as glucose falls; a falling corrected sodium is an early warning sign of cerebral oedema.

stem: A 3-year-old girl (weight 14 kg) is brought into a regional ED resuscitation bay by ambulance after 6 hours of pallor, lethargy, and refusing fluids. Paramedics noted a narrow-complex regular tachycardia at 268 bpm on monitor with poor peripheral perfusion. She has had one previous documented episode of supraventricular tachycardia at age 18 months terminated by adenosine. On arrival she is pale, mottled, and increasingly drowsy. She has poor peripheral pulses, no palpable femoral, and is barely rousable. IV access has just been obtained in the right antecubital fossa with a 22-gauge cannula on the second attempt. The rhythm strip from the ambulance shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia with no visible P waves at a rate of 270 bpm; QRS duration 70 ms. Vagal manoeuvres (ice to face) were trialled at home by the mother and in the ambulance without effect. No drugs have yet been given in ED. The defibrillator is at the bedside with paediatric pads applied.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 270 bpm narrow complex, RR 36 /min, SpO2 92% on 6 L/min Hudson mask, BP unrecordable on cuff, T 37.0 deg C
data_table.Examination: Pale, mottled to mid-thigh, capillary refill 5-6 seconds, GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5), no femoral pulse palpable, no murmurs audible
data_table.ECG: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia 270 bpm, QRS 70 ms, no visible P waves, no delta wave evident
data_table.Medications: Nil given in ED, vagal manoeuvres tried unsuccessfully
data_table.History: Prior SVT terminated by adenosine age 18 months, no structural heart disease known, no recent illness

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Rapid IV adenosine 0.1 mg/kg push followed by a 10 mL saline flush via the right antecubital cannula
options.rationale: Adenosine is the agent of choice in stable SVT, but this child is in haemodynamically unstable SVT (unrecordable BP, GCS 11, no femoral pulse), which requires immediate synchronised DC cardioversion, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7 and RCH CPG: Tachyarrhythmias.

options.text: Synchronised DC cardioversion at 1 J/kg with procedural sedation if no immediate delay
options.rationale: Unstable SVT in a child with shock requires immediate synchronised DC cardioversion at 1 J/kg (then 2 J/kg if unsuccessful), with sedation only if it does not delay shock delivery, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7 and AHA PALS.

options.text: Unsynchronised defibrillation at 4 J/kg as for ventricular fibrillation
options.rationale: Unsynchronised defibrillation is for pulseless rhythms (VF, pulseless VT); delivering an unsynchronised shock on a perfusing rhythm risks induction of VF, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

options.text: IV amiodarone 5 mg/kg loading over 20-60 minutes before any electrical therapy
options.rationale: Amiodarone is considered in refractory or recurrent SVT in stable patients or after cardioversion; it is not appropriate before electrical therapy in unstable SVT, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

options.text: IV verapamil 0.1 mg/kg as a slow push to break the re-entrant tachycardia
options.rationale: Verapamil is contraindicated in children under 1 year and is unsafe in any child with poor cardiac output because of profound hypotension and cardiac arrest risk, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

explanation.summary: Paediatric SVT with shock = immediate synchronised cardioversion at 1 J/kg, escalating to 2 J/kg. Stable SVT = vagal manoeuvres then adenosine 0.1 mg/kg, then 0.2 mg/kg. Avoid verapamil in young children and avoid unsynchronised shocks on perfusing rhythms.
key_points: Unstable = shock, altered mental state, hypotension; cardiovert immediately
1 J/kg synchronised, then 2 J/kg if unsuccessful
Adenosine 0.1 mg/kg then 0.2 mg/kg in stable SVT
Verapamil contraindicated under 1 year and in shock
Amiodarone is second-line for refractory cases
explanation.pearls: If rate is over 220 bpm in an infant or over 180 bpm in a child with abrupt onset and no P waves, think SVT, not sinus tachycardia.

stem: A 14-month-old boy (weight 11 kg) presents to a regional ED with 12 hours of barking cough, hoarse cry, and worsening stridor at rest. In ED he received nebulised adrenaline 5 mg (1:1000) and oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg 45 minutes ago. He had transient improvement for 20 minutes but is now again stridulous at rest, with worsening recessions, increased work of breathing, and intermittent quiet episodes followed by paradoxical chest movement. He is restless and difficult to console; oxygen saturations are dropping. No drooling, no preceding fever above 38.0 deg C, no foreign body history. Fully immunised. The anaesthetic registrar and ENT registrar have been paged and are 5 minutes away. The team is preparing the difficult airway trolley.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 48 /min, SpO2 88% on 8 L/min Hudson mask, BP 102/66 mmHg, T 37.6 deg C
data_table.Examination: Marked biphasic stridor, severe sternal and subcostal recessions, intermittent paradoxical chest wall movement, restless and not consolable, cyanosis around the lips
data_table.Treatment given: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg x1 (45 min ago), oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (45 min ago)
data_table.History: Barking cough 12 h, no drooling, no high fever, fully immunised, no FB history

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Wait 30 minutes for the dexamethasone to take full effect before any further intervention
options.rationale: He has impending respiratory failure (hypoxia, exhaustion, paradoxical breathing); waiting passively is unsafe, as per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline 5 mg immediately and prepare for theatre intubation by anaesthetics and ENT
options.rationale: Repeat nebulised adrenaline can be given every 20-30 minutes and is reasonable as a bridge, but the dominant priority once the child has signs of impending respiratory failure is definitive airway in theatre rather than just repeat adrenaline alone, as per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula oxygen 2 L/kg/min and reassess at 30 minutes without airway team involvement
options.rationale: HFNC may have a role in some upper airway pathology but it does not bypass critical narrowing and should not delay airway team involvement when impending failure is present, as per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline 5 mg as a bridge while transferring to theatre for inhalational induction and intubation by anaesthetics and ENT, with smaller-than-predicted ETT and difficult airway trolley
options.rationale: Severe croup unresponsive to initial adrenaline and steroid with signs of impending respiratory failure requires definitive airway management in theatre with inhalational induction, smaller-than-predicted ETT, and ENT standby for surgical airway; repeat adrenaline is used as a bridge, as per RCH CPG: Croup and APLS Australia.

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation in ED with rocuronium 1 mg/kg and an age-appropriate sized cuffed ETT
options.rationale: RSI with paralysis in severe croup risks losing the airway when bag-mask ventilation may be difficult; inhalational induction preserving spontaneous ventilation in theatre with ENT standby is preferred, as per APLS Australia.

explanation.summary: Severe croup unresponsive to nebulised adrenaline and dexamethasone with signs of impending respiratory failure (hypoxia, exhaustion, decreasing air entry, paradoxical breathing) requires definitive airway in theatre. Inhalational induction with preserved spontaneous ventilation is preferred over paralysis-based RSI. ENT must be on standby for a potential surgical airway, and a smaller-than-predicted ETT should be selected.
key_points: Repeat nebulised adrenaline every 20-30 minutes as needed as a bridge
Definitive airway in theatre when impending failure
Inhalational induction; avoid paralysis
Use ETT one size smaller than predicted
ENT for emergency surgical airway
explanation.pearls: Quiet stridor with exhaustion is more ominous than loud stridor with vigorous breathing.

stem: A 13-year-old girl (weight 52 kg) is brought to ED by her parents after they found an empty 100-tablet bottle of paracetamol 500 mg in her room and a written note. She admits to taking tablets in two separate episodes over the past 18 hours: she states she took about 30 tablets approximately 14 hours ago after an argument with her sister, then a further 20 tablets about 4 hours ago when she decided she wanted to die. She denies co-ingestion of other tablets, alcohol, or recreational drugs and there are no other empty packets in the home. She has no chronic medical illness, has a normal body mass index, is not on enzyme-inducing drugs (no carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampicin, or isoniazid), and there is no chronic alcohol use, eating disorder, or malnutrition that would raise glutathione-depletion risk. She is fully alert with no nausea or right upper quadrant pain. The on-call mental health team has been notified and is on the way. A paracetamol level taken on arrival (now 4 hours after the second ingestion and 18 hours after the first) is 320 micromol/L. ALT is 38 U/L and INR is 1.1; creatinine is 64 micromol/L; bicarbonate, lactate, and glucose are all normal. She has not vomited and has not yet received activated charcoal or N-acetylcysteine. The on-call clinical toxicologist has been contacted by phone.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 bpm, RR 16 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 118/72 mmHg, T 36.8 deg C
data_table.Examination: Alert and oriented, no jaundice, abdomen soft and non-tender, no asterixis, mood low but engaged
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol 320 micromol/L (4 h post second ingestion / 18 h post first), ALT 38 U/L, INR 1.1, creatinine 64 micromol/L, glucose 5.2 mmol/L, VBG normal
data_table.Ingestion: Approx 30 x 500 mg paracetamol 14 h ago, then approx 20 x 500 mg paracetamol 4 h ago; total ~25 g ingested in staggered fashion
data_table.Other: No co-ingestants, no enzyme inducers, no chronic illness

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management decision regarding N-acetylcysteine?

options.text: Plot the 320 micromol/L level on the paracetamol treatment nomogram at the 4-hour line, and withhold N-acetylcysteine as it falls below the treatment line
options.rationale: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies only to single acute ingestions with a known time; in staggered ingestion or unknown time it cannot be used, and a low nomogram level does not exclude toxicity, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness and RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning.

options.text: Withhold N-acetylcysteine because ALT and INR are normal, and arrange repeat bloods in 8 hours to reassess
options.rationale: ALT and INR rise late (often after 24-36 hours); waiting for derangement in a staggered overdose of >200 mg/kg risks missing the treatment window where NAC is most effective, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

options.text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine 200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours immediately, irrespective of the nomogram level, given the staggered ingestion and total dose >200 mg/kg
options.rationale: Staggered paracetamol ingestion >200 mg/kg (or >10 g in adults) over <24 hours mandates immediate full-course IV N-acetylcysteine regardless of the nomogram level; the nomogram does not apply, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness, RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning, and the Australian/New Zealand consensus statement on paracetamol overdose.
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand consensus statement: paracetamol poisoning

options.text: Give activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally and reassess paracetamol level in 4 hours before deciding on N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is only useful within 1-2 hours of ingestion; here the earlier ingestion is 18 hours old and the more recent one is 4 hours old, so charcoal has minimal benefit, and N-acetylcysteine should not be delayed, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

options.text: Commence oral methionine 2.5 g every 4 hours for 4 doses as an alternative to IV N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: Oral methionine is not used in current Australian practice for paracetamol overdose; IV N-acetylcysteine is the standard antidote, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

explanation.summary: Staggered paracetamol overdose (more than one ingestion within a 24-hour window) renders the Rumack-Matthew nomogram invalid. If the total dose exceeds 200 mg/kg or 10 g, give full-course IV N-acetylcysteine regardless of the level. Charcoal is only useful within 1-2 hours of ingestion. Normal ALT and INR at presentation do not exclude developing toxicity.
key_points: Nomogram applies only to single ingestions with known time
Staggered ingestion >200 mg/kg or >10 g -> immediate NAC
NAC regimen: 200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h
Repeat paracetamol, ALT, INR at 8 h to decide on extension of NAC
Charcoal only if presentation within 1-2 h of ingestion
explanation.pearls: Massive ingestion (>30 g or paracetamol >1000 micromol/L at 4 h) may need double-dose NAC and dialysis consideration.

sources.label: Australian and New Zealand consensus statement: paracetamol poisoning

stem: A 7-year-old boy (estimated weight 24 kg) arrives in a tertiary trauma bay 65 minutes after being struck by a car as a pedestrian. Prehospital observations were HR 152, BP 76/40, GCS 14. He received 10 mL/kg of 0.9% sodium chloride en route. FAST scan in the trauma bay is positive for free fluid in Morison's pouch and pelvis; he has an obvious right femur deformity in a traction splint, abrasions over the right chest wall, and bruising across the abdomen consistent with a seatbelt mechanism. He is now drowsy with a GCS of 13, cool peripheries, and ongoing hypotension despite the prehospital crystalloid bolus. The on-call surgeon and interventional radiologist have been called. The blood bank has a paediatric massive transfusion pack available within 8 minutes. No tranexamic acid has yet been given. He has no known coagulopathy and no anticoagulants.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156 bpm, RR 32 /min, SpO2 96% on 10 L/min non-rebreather, BP 78/44 mmHg, T 35.6 deg C, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Bruised abdomen with seatbelt sign, positive FAST (Morison + pelvis), right femur deformity, capillary refill 4 s, cool peripheries to knees
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L, lactate 4.6 mmol/L, base excess -8, INR 1.4, fibrinogen 1.6 g/L, platelets 198 x10^9/L, ionised Ca 1.05 mmol/L
data_table.Time: 65 minutes since injury, 10 mL/kg saline given prehospital, no blood products yet
data_table.History: No anticoagulants, no bleeding diathesis, fully immunised

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in haemodynamic resuscitation?

options.text: Activate the paediatric massive transfusion protocol, give IV tranexamic acid 15 mg/kg (max 1 g) over 10 minutes, and transfuse PRBC 10 mL/kg as the initial bolus
options.rationale: Paediatric trauma with shock, positive FAST and lactic acidosis after 10 mL/kg crystalloid meets MTP activation criteria; TXA is indicated when given within 3 hours of injury at 15 mg/kg (max 1 g) followed by an infusion, and initial PRBC bolus is 10 mL/kg, as per CRASH-3/CRASH-2 paediatric data and RCH CPG: Trauma resuscitation.
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Trauma Society Paediatric Massive Transfusion Guidelines

options.text: Give a further 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride and reassess before activating MTP
options.rationale: Large-volume crystalloid worsens dilutional coagulopathy and acidosis in haemorrhagic shock; blood products should be the next resuscitation fluid in a trauma patient with positive FAST and ongoing shock, as per RCH CPG: Trauma resuscitation.

options.text: Transfuse PRBC 20 mL/kg alone and withhold tranexamic acid until thromboelastography results are available
options.rationale: TXA should be given empirically within 3 hours of injury in trauma with significant bleeding; waiting for viscoelastic testing delays a time-critical intervention, as per CRASH-2 paediatric extrapolation and the ANZ paediatric MTP guidance.
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Trauma Society Paediatric Massive Transfusion Guidelines

options.text: Withhold tranexamic acid as it is contraindicated in paediatric trauma and give cryoprecipitate 10 mL/kg first to correct fibrinogen
options.rationale: TXA is not contraindicated in paediatric trauma; it is recommended within 3 hours of injury. Cryoprecipitate is used for fibrinogen <1.5 g/L but is not the first product in the MTP, as per ANZ Paediatric Massive Transfusion Guidelines.
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Trauma Society Paediatric Massive Transfusion Guidelines

options.text: Commence peripheral noradrenaline at 0.1 microg/kg/min to maintain perfusion and defer blood products until SBP is restored
options.rationale: Vasopressors as primary treatment of haemorrhagic shock mask ongoing bleeding and worsen tissue perfusion; volume replacement with blood products is the priority, as per RCH CPG: Trauma resuscitation.

explanation.summary: Paediatric haemorrhagic shock not responding to a 10-20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, with positive FAST or known source of bleeding, requires MTP activation, balanced product resuscitation (PRBC:FFP:platelets ~1:1:1, initial PRBC 10 mL/kg), and empirical TXA within 3 hours of injury. Avoid further crystalloid where blood is available.
key_points: MTP activation: shock unresponsive to 10-20 mL/kg crystalloid or known major haemorrhage
TXA 15 mg/kg (max 1 g) over 10 min, then 2 mg/kg/h for 8 h
Aim ionised calcium >1.1 mmol/L; give calcium with each unit
Hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy = lethal triad
Surgical or radiological source control as soon as possible
explanation.pearls: Children compensate longer then decompensate suddenly; do not be falsely reassured by a normal blood pressure early.

sources.label: Australian and New Zealand Trauma Society Paediatric Massive Transfusion Guidelines

stem: A 5-month-old male infant (not yet sitting independently or rolling consistently) is brought to ED by his mother for a frenulum tear noted during a feed. Mother states he banged his face on the cot 2 days ago but the lip injury was only seen today. The father is a step-father who was alone with the infant on the day of the injury. There is no past medical history of bleeding or bruising disorders. On examination there is a torn upper lip frenulum with surrounding bruising on the upper gum and a small yellow bruise over the left pinna. He is otherwise alert, well-perfused, feeding adequately on review, and has no acute distress. A previous ED record from 6 weeks ago notes a presentation for a left thigh bruise of unclear mechanism, dismissed as a possible cot-rail injury at the time. The Department of Communities and Justice has not previously been involved.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138 bpm, RR 36 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 90/56 mmHg, T 36.9 deg C
data_table.Examination: Torn upper lip frenulum with gum bruising, small yellow bruise over left pinna, no other external bruising on full skin exam, no torticollis, no head swelling, anterior fontanelle soft, retinal exam not performed
data_table.Development: Not yet rolling consistently, not sitting independently; pre-mobile per WHO milestones
data_table.Past presentation: ED visit 6 weeks ago for left thigh bruise, dismissed as cot-rail injury
data_table.Social: Mother and step-father carers, no prior child protection involvement

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure mother that frenulum tears are common in this age group and arrange GP review in 1 week
options.rationale: A frenulum tear in a pre-mobile infant is a sentinel injury for non-accidental injury and never warrants outpatient reassurance alone, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study guidance.
source_refs: RACGP: Identifying and responding to child abuse and neglect

options.text: Send coagulation studies and full blood count, and discharge home if results are normal
options.rationale: Coagulation studies are part of the workup to exclude bleeding diathesis but a normal screen does not exclude inflicted injury; discharge home without protective action is unsafe, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

options.text: Refer to outpatient paediatrics in 2 weeks for assessment, with no statutory notification at this stage
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting is required at the time of clinical suspicion, not deferred to outpatient review; medical practitioners are mandatory reporters under state child protection legislation, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse.
source_refs: RACGP: Identifying and responding to child abuse and neglect

options.text: Discharge with father supervised by mother only, after a verbal safety plan with mother
options.rationale: Discharging a pre-mobile infant with a sentinel injury without a child protection risk assessment or statutory notification does not meet the duty of care or mandatory reporting obligations, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

options.text: Admit the infant, make a mandatory child protection notification to the statutory body, arrange a skeletal survey, fundoscopy by ophthalmology, and a non-contrast CT head; defer ongoing investigation discussion until the child is in a safe environment
options.rationale: A frenulum tear or bruise in a pre-mobile infant (TEN-4 FACESp rule) is a high-risk sentinel injury for inflicted injury; the standard response is admission, mandatory notification, skeletal survey (NAI series), fundoscopy for retinal haemorrhages, and CT head, with paediatric protection team involvement, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse and the RACGP child protection resource.
source_refs: RACGP: Identifying and responding to child abuse and neglect

explanation.summary: Sentinel injuries in pre-mobile infants (TEN-4 FACESp: Torso, Ear, Neck bruising; Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheek, Eyelid, Sclera, plus any patterned injury; under 4 months any bruising) require admission, mandatory child protection notification, skeletal survey, fundoscopy for retinal haemorrhages, and CT head to assess for occult abusive head trauma.
key_points: Bruising/frenulum injury in a pre-mobile infant is NAI until proven otherwise
Mandatory reporting is statutory in all Australian states/territories
Skeletal survey + fundoscopy + CT head are minimum imaging
Coagulation screen to exclude bleeding diathesis is adjunctive
Involve paediatric child protection team and social work
explanation.pearls: Those who don't cruise don't bruise: any bruise in a non-mobile infant warrants child protection assessment.

sources.label: RACGP: Identifying and responding to child abuse and neglect

stem: A 2-year-old boy (weight 12 kg) is brought to a regional ED 90 minutes after pulling a kettle of boiling water onto himself. On primary survey his airway is patent with no soot or singed nasal hair, he is crying, and his vital signs are stable. Cooling with cool running water was applied for 20 minutes at the scene. Burn assessment using a paediatric Lund-Browder chart shows partial-thickness scalds covering the anterior chest, anterior abdomen, anterior aspect of the right arm and forearm, and the anterior aspect of the right thigh; the total estimated body surface area involved is 18%. There are no full-thickness areas and no circumferential burns. He has received IV morphine for analgesia, simple wound dressings with cling film, and one peripheral IV cannula has been placed. No IV fluids have yet been started. His baseline urine output has not been measured. The retrieval service has been contacted and a paediatric burns unit bed is being arranged.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138 bpm, RR 30 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 102/64 mmHg, T 36.8 deg C
data_table.Examination: 18% TBSA partial-thickness scald (anterior chest, abdomen, right arm/forearm, right anterior thigh), no full-thickness, no airway involvement
data_table.Maintenance calc: Weight 12 kg => maintenance = 100 mL x 10 kg + 50 mL x 2 kg = 1100 mL/24 h
data_table.Resus access: 1 peripheral IV cannula, no fluids running yet, no urinary catheter yet

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate fluid prescription for the next 24 hours?

options.text: Hartmann's solution 12 mL/kg/h (864 mL over 24 h) total, including maintenance, titrated to urine output of 0.5 mL/kg/h
options.rationale: This rate underestimates the Parkland requirement and does not separate maintenance from resuscitation fluid; the modified Parkland formula in children adds maintenance on top of the burns resuscitation fluid, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines and RCH CPG: Burns.

options.text: Hartmann's solution at 3 mL/kg/% TBSA (648 mL) over 24 h, with half in the first 8 h and half in the next 16 h, PLUS maintenance fluid 1100 mL/24 h (5% dextrose with 0.45% sodium chloride), aiming for urine output 1 mL/kg/h
options.rationale: The modified Parkland formula in Australian paediatric burns is 3-4 mL/kg/% TBSA of Hartmann's over 24 hours (half in the first 8 hours from time of injury), PLUS maintenance fluid on top, titrated to a urine output of 1 mL/kg/h in children, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines and RCH CPG: Burns.

options.text: 0.9% sodium chloride at 4 mL/kg/% TBSA over 24 h with NO additional maintenance fluid, aiming for urine output 0.5 mL/kg/h
options.rationale: Hartmann's solution is preferred over 0.9% saline because of the chloride load; in children maintenance is given separately, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines.

options.text: 5% albumin 1 mL/kg/% TBSA over the first 8 h as primary resuscitation fluid, with crystalloid maintenance on top
options.rationale: Colloid is generally added after the first 8-12 hours in selected paediatric burns; crystalloid (Hartmann's) is the primary resuscitation fluid for the first 24 hours, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines.

options.text: Hartmann's solution at 6 mL/kg/% TBSA (1296 mL) over the first 8 h followed by maintenance only, aiming for urine output 2 mL/kg/h
options.rationale: Over-resuscitation (volumes well above 4 mL/kg/% TBSA) causes burn oedema and abdominal compartment syndrome; the target urine output in children is 1 mL/kg/h not 2 mL/kg/h, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines.

explanation.summary: Paediatric burn resuscitation uses the modified Parkland formula: 3-4 mL/kg/% TBSA Hartmann's over 24 hours from time of injury (half in first 8 hours), PLUS maintenance fluid on top. Surface area is estimated using a paediatric Lund-Browder chart (not adult Rule of Nines). Target urine output is 1 mL/kg/h in children (2 mL/kg/h in infants <30 kg can be appropriate depending on protocol).
key_points: Parkland (paeds): 3-4 mL/kg/% TBSA Hartmann's over 24 h, half in first 8 h
Maintenance fluid is ADDITIONAL, not included
Use Lund-Browder, not Rule of Nines, in children
Aim urine output ~1 mL/kg/h
Adjust starting clock to time of injury, not time of presentation
explanation.pearls: Hartmann's is preferred over 0.9% saline because of the chloride load and risk of hyperchloraemic acidosis.

stem: A 4-year-old unimmunised boy presents to a regional ED with 6 hours of high fever to 39.4 deg C, painful drooling, refusal to swallow, and a muffled hot-potato voice. He is sitting forward on his mother's lap in a tripod posture with his neck extended. There has been no preceding barking cough and no hoarseness; the parents describe rapid onset over a single afternoon. He has no history of choking on a small object, no recent dental work, and no preceding bacterial pharyngitis. He has not had any nebulised adrenaline or steroid. The ED consultant has asked the team to avoid causing distress, to call anaesthetics and ENT immediately, and to keep the child on the mother's lap. He is fully alert but anxious. On overall appearance he looks toxic.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148 bpm, RR 32 /min, SpO2 95% on room air, BP 102/64 mmHg, T 39.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Tripoding, drooling, muffled voice, mild inspiratory stridor, no barking cough, no hoarseness, neck extended, anxious but alert
data_table.History: Unimmunised, no preceding URTI, rapid onset over hours, no FB choking event, no dental procedure
data_table.Treatments given: Nil so far; team avoiding distress and calling airway team

lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Acute epiglottitis caused by Haemophilus influenzae type b in an unimmunised child
options.rationale: The combination of rapid onset, high fever, drooling, muffled (hot-potato) voice, tripoding without barking cough in an unimmunised child is classic for epiglottitis, most commonly Hib in unimmunised children, as per RCH CPG: Epiglottitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Viral laryngotracheobronchitis (croup) caused by parainfluenza virus type 1
options.rationale: Croup typically has a preceding URTI, barking cough, hoarseness, and inspiratory stridor without drooling or a toxic appearance; absence of barking cough and presence of drooling argue against it, as per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Inhaled foreign body lodged at the carina with secondary inflammation
options.rationale: FB aspiration typically follows a witnessed choking event with sudden cough and unilateral wheeze; absence of a choking history and presence of fever and toxic appearance make this unlikely, as per RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation.

options.text: Bacterial tracheitis as a complication of preceding viral croup with Staphylococcus aureus superinfection
options.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis usually follows several days of croup-like illness with worsening toxicity and copious purulent tracheal secretions; the absence of preceding croup phase argues against it, as per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Retropharyngeal abscess secondary to a preceding bacterial pharyngitis or dental infection
options.rationale: Retropharyngeal abscess typically presents with neck stiffness or torticollis and a preceding URTI or dental focus, sometimes with reluctance to extend the neck; the rapid onset and tripoding favour epiglottitis, as per BMJ Best Practice: Retropharyngeal abscess.

explanation.summary: Acute upper airway obstruction in a child requires rapid differentiation between croup, epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis, foreign body, and retropharyngeal abscess. Epiglottitis features rapid onset, high fever, drooling, muffled voice, tripoding, no barking cough. In an unimmunised child Hib is the leading pathogen.
key_points: Epiglottitis: 4 Ds (drooling, dysphagia, distress, dysphonia), tripoding, no cough
Croup: preceding URTI, barking cough, hoarseness, stridor
Bacterial tracheitis: croup-like prodrome then toxic decline
Foreign body: sudden choking event, unilateral signs
Retropharyngeal abscess: neck stiffness, dysphagia, preceding URTI or dental
explanation.pearls: Keep the child calm on the parent's lap, do not examine the throat, call anaesthetics and ENT, and plan inhalational induction in theatre.

stem: A 6-year-old boy (weight 22 kg) with newly diagnosed T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is admitted to the paediatric oncology ward 12 hours after starting induction with high-dose steroid and his first dose of rasburicase. The bone marrow blast count at diagnosis was 92%, white cell count 142 x10^9/L, with bulky mediastinal disease on imaging. He has been on hyperhydration with 2.5 L/m2/day of isotonic fluid without potassium since admission. Over the last 4 hours he has become increasingly nauseated and reports muscle cramps and tingling around the mouth, and is now passing only small volumes of dark urine. A repeat biochemistry panel ordered after the nursing staff noted reduced urine output shows the changes below. A 12-lead ECG performed because of the potassium result shows peaked T waves in the precordial leads and a PR interval of 220 ms. The renal registrar has been called.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 bpm, RR 22 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 124/78 mmHg, T 37.0 deg C
data_table.Examination: Mild Chvostek sign, no carpopedal spasm, no peripheral oedema, abdomen soft, no flank tenderness
data_table.Investigations: K 6.9 mmol/L, urate 0.62 mmol/L, phosphate 3.4 mmol/L, corrected calcium 1.78 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 48), urea 12.2 mmol/L, pH 7.31, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, urine output 0.3 mL/kg/h
data_table.ECG: Peaked T waves V2-V5, PR 220 ms, QRS 90 ms, no widening yet
data_table.Treatment so far: Hyperhydration without K, rasburicase 12 h ago, no calcium given, no insulin-glucose, no salbutamol

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate next step in management?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg as the first agent to shift potassium intracellularly, then reassess ECG in 15 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol shifts potassium but acts more slowly than calcium and does not protect the myocardium; cardiac membrane stabilisation with IV calcium is the first step when there are ECG changes, as per RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia and APLS Australia.

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg IV bolus as primary therapy to drive potassium intracellularly
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate has a limited and variable effect on potassium and worsens hypocalcaemia (already 1.78 mmol/L), risking tetany and arrhythmia; it is not first-line, as per APLS Australia Electrolyte Emergencies.

options.text: IV 10% calcium gluconate 0.5 mL/kg (max 20 mL) over 5-10 minutes with continuous ECG monitoring, then commence insulin-dextrose infusion (0.1 unit/kg insulin with 0.5 g/kg dextrose) and refer for urgent dialysis
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes (peaked T waves, prolonged PR) requires immediate myocardial protection with IV calcium gluconate, followed by potassium-shifting therapy (insulin-dextrose, salbutamol) and definitive removal via dialysis given the rising creatinine, oliguria and hyperphosphataemia of TLS, as per RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia, RCH CPG: Tumour lysis syndrome, and APLS Australia.

options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate 1 g/kg as the first intervention while ECG changes resolve spontaneously
options.rationale: Resin binders are slow-acting and unsuitable as the immediate intervention in hyperkalaemia with ECG changes; they also carry GI necrosis risk, as per RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia.

options.text: IV furosemide 1 mg/kg as primary therapy to enhance renal potassium excretion in this oliguric child
options.rationale: Loop diuretics require functional kidneys and are not effective in established oliguric acute kidney injury due to TLS; cardiac protection and definitive removal (dialysis) are needed, as per RCH CPG: Tumour lysis syndrome.

explanation.summary: Tumour lysis syndrome (high urate, high phosphate, high potassium, low calcium, AKI) is a classic emergency in high-risk haematological malignancies. Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is treated in three steps: stabilise the myocardium (IV calcium gluconate), shift potassium (insulin-dextrose, salbutamol, bicarbonate selectively), and remove potassium (dialysis when AKI is established, oliguric, or refractory).
key_points: Calcium gluconate 10% 0.5 mL/kg IV over 5-10 min for ECG changes
Insulin 0.1 unit/kg with dextrose 0.5 g/kg
Salbutamol nebulised 5 mg as adjunct
Dialysis criteria in TLS: oliguric AKI, K >7 refractory, severe hyperphosphataemia, fluid overload
Avoid potassium-containing fluids; rasburicase for high urate
explanation.pearls: Hypocalcaemia in TLS is precipitation as calcium phosphate; correct calcium cautiously only if symptomatic because raising it worsens calcium phosphate deposition.

stem: A 6-day-old male term infant (birth weight 3.4 kg, now 3.2 kg) is brought to a regional ED by his parents with poor feeding, lethargy, and grey colour over the past 12 hours. He was born at 39+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery, with Apgars 9 and 9, and discharged from the maternity unit at 36 hours after a normal newborn examination. The newborn pulse oximetry screen at the birth hospital was reportedly normal at 24 hours. There is no family history of congenital heart disease. On arrival in ED he is grey-mottled, with cool peripheries and weak femoral pulses bilaterally; the four-limb blood pressures show a right arm of 78/50 mmHg and a right leg of 52/30 mmHg. Pre-ductal SpO2 (right hand) is 92%, post-ductal SpO2 (foot) is 78%. He has a hyperdynamic precordium and a single loud second heart sound with no audible murmur. The chest x-ray shows cardiomegaly with pulmonary plethora. Bedside echo by the visiting paediatric cardiology fellow on tele-link demonstrates a critical coarctation of the aorta with a closing ductus arteriosus. The retrieval service is en route.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178 bpm, RR 64 /min, SpO2 pre-ductal 92% / post-ductal 78%, BP RA 78/50, RL 52/30, T 36.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Grey-mottled, cool to mid-thigh, weak femoral pulses, hyperdynamic precordium, single loud S2, no murmur, hepatomegaly 3 cm
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.18 lactate 6.2 mmol/L base excess -12, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, Na 136 mmol/L, K 4.6 mmol/L, Hb 168 g/L; CXR cardiomegaly + pulmonary plethora; echo: critical coarctation with closing PDA
data_table.Differential considered and ruled out by: Sepsis (in parallel workup), hypoglycaemia (treated), inborn error of metabolism (less likely with structural finding)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Indomethacin 0.2 mg/kg IV to close the patent ductus arteriosus and improve systemic perfusion
options.rationale: Closing the duct in a duct-dependent systemic circulation (critical coarctation, interrupted aortic arch, hypoplastic left heart) will precipitate cardiovascular collapse; the goal is to keep the duct open, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: Adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid IV push to terminate suspected supraventricular tachycardia and improve cardiac output
options.rationale: There is no narrow-complex tachycardia at >220 bpm with absent P waves; the picture is duct-dependent systemic shock, not SVT, and adenosine has no benefit, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

options.text: Sildenafil 0.5 mg/kg orally to reduce pulmonary vascular resistance and improve oxygenation
options.rationale: Sildenafil is used in persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, not duct-dependent systemic lesions; it does not maintain ductal patency, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: IV prostaglandin E1 infusion starting at 10-50 nanog/kg/min (commonly 0.05 microg/kg/min), with airway and apnoea management readiness
options.rationale: Duct-dependent systemic circulation with a closing PDA is reopened with prostaglandin E1 infusion; the starting dose is 0.01-0.05 microg/kg/min titrated to effect, with airway support readiness because of apnoea risk, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: Commence high-dose oxygen at FiO2 1.0 via head-box to maximise systemic saturations and reduce hypoxia-driven acidosis
options.rationale: High FiO2 in duct-dependent systemic circulation can promote ductal closure (oxygen is a stimulus for closure) and worsen the situation; target pre-ductal SpO2 ~85-92% rather than 100%, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

explanation.summary: Neonatal collapse between days 3-14 with shock, pre/post-ductal saturation differential and weak femoral pulses should be assumed to be duct-dependent congenital heart disease until proven otherwise. Treatment is IV prostaglandin E1 infusion to reopen the duct. Avoid high FiO2 and indomethacin. Be ready for apnoea, fever, and hypotension as PGE1 side effects.
key_points: Duct-dependent systemic: coarctation, IAA, HLHS, critical AS
PGE1 0.01-0.05 microg/kg/min titrated to effect
Apnoea risk: anticipate intubation
Target SpO2 ~85-92%; avoid 100% oxygen
Retrieve early to cardiac centre
explanation.pearls: Differential SpO2 with lower foot than hand is highly suggestive of a duct-dependent systemic or mixing lesion; check four-limb BPs.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

stem: A 14-year-old boy (weight 60 kg) with a mechanical mitral valve replacement on long-term anticoagulation is brought to ED 90 minutes after coming off his bicycle without a helmet. He had a brief loss of consciousness at the scene and one episode of vomiting. He is on dabigatran 110 mg twice daily (switched from warfarin 6 months ago due to INR variability); his last dose was 2 hours pre-injury. On arrival his GCS is 13 (E3 V4 M6) and dropping; he has a scalp haematoma over the right parietal area, equal but sluggish pupils, and a non-contrast CT head shows an acute right-sided subdural haematoma with 6 mm of midline shift. Neurosurgery plans urgent operative evacuation within the hour. INR is 1.2; aPTT 58 s; the thrombin time is markedly prolonged. He has no other major injuries on secondary survey.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92 bpm, RR 18 /min, SpO2 98% on 4 L/min nasal prongs, BP 132/82 mmHg, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Right parietal scalp haematoma, equal sluggish pupils, no focal limb weakness, no other injuries
data_table.Imaging: Non-contrast CT head: right subdural haematoma with 6 mm midline shift; neurosurgery for urgent evacuation
data_table.Anticoagulant: Dabigatran 110 mg BD, last dose 2 hours before injury (so ~3.5 h pre-decision)
data_table.Coags: INR 1.2, aPTT 58 s, thrombin time markedly prolonged

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate reversal strategy before craniotomy?

options.text: IV vitamin K 10 mg plus prothrombinex-VF 50 IU/kg as for warfarin reversal
options.rationale: Vitamin K and prothrombinex are the warfarin reversal regimen; dabigatran is a direct thrombin inhibitor and is not reversed by vitamin K, as per ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines and Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular.
source_refs: ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines

options.text: Andexanet alfa 400 mg IV bolus then 4 mg/min infusion as specific reversal
options.rationale: Andexanet alfa reverses factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban), not dabigatran (a direct thrombin inhibitor); the specific antidote here is idarucizumab, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular.

options.text: Fresh frozen plasma 15 mL/kg infused over 1 hour as the primary reversal
options.rationale: FFP does not reliably reverse direct thrombin inhibitors and volume-loads the patient; specific antidote idarucizumab is preferred when available, as per ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines.
source_refs: ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines

options.text: Recombinant activated factor VII 90 microg/kg IV as a universal haemostatic agent
options.rationale: Recombinant FVIIa is off-label, carries thrombotic risk especially in a patient with a mechanical valve, and is not a specific reversal agent for dabigatran, as per ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines.
source_refs: ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines

options.text: Idarucizumab 5 g IV (given as two 2.5 g doses 15 minutes apart) as specific dabigatran reversal, with neurosurgery proceeding immediately afterwards
options.rationale: Idarucizumab is a specific monoclonal antibody fragment that immediately neutralises dabigatran and is indicated for life-threatening bleeding (including intracranial haemorrhage) or urgent surgery in patients on dabigatran, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular and ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines.
source_refs: ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines

explanation.summary: Anticoagulant reversal in life-threatening bleeding is agent-specific: warfarin = vitamin K + prothrombinex (or FFP if PCC unavailable); dabigatran = idarucizumab 5 g IV; factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban) = andexanet alfa where available, otherwise prothrombinex 50 IU/kg. A markedly prolonged thrombin time confirms dabigatran activity.
key_points: Warfarin -> vitamin K + prothrombinex-VF 50 IU/kg
Dabigatran -> idarucizumab 5 g IV (2 x 2.5 g)
Apixaban/rivaroxaban -> andexanet alfa; alternative prothrombinex
FFP is volume-heavy and slow; not first choice when PCC/antidote available
Thrombin time is sensitive to dabigatran effect
explanation.pearls: Always check the last dose time and renal function for DOACs; dabigatran is renally cleared and may persist longer in AKI.

sources.label: ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines
